instruction: What are some challenges developers face when designing a reliable data system?
output: CHAPTER 1 Reliable, Scalable, and Maintainable Applications The Internet was done so well that most people think of it as a natural resource like the Pacific Ocean, rather than something that was man-made. When was the last time a tech‐ nology with a scale like that was so error-free? —Alan Kay, in interview with Dr Dobb’s Journal (2012) Many applications today are data-intensive, as opposed to compute-intensive. Raw CPU power is rarely a limiting factor for these applications—bigger problems are usually the amount of data, the complexity of data, and the speed at which it is changing. A data-intensive application is typically built from standard building blocks that pro‐ vide commonly needed functionality. For example, many applications need to: • Store data so that they, or another application, can find it again later (databases) • Remember the result of an expensive operation, to speed up reads (caches) • Allow users to search data by keyword or filter it in various ways (search indexes) • Send a message to another process, to be handled asynchronously (stream pro‐ cessing) • Periodically crunch a large amount of accumulated data (batch processing) If that sounds painfully obvious, that’s just because these data systems are such a suc‐ cessful abstraction: we use them all the time without thinking too much. When build‐ ing an application, most engineers wouldn’t dream of writing a new data storage engine from scratch, because databases are a perfectly good tool for the job. 3 But reality is not that simple. There are many database systems with different charac‐ teristics, because different applications have different requirements. There are vari‐ ous approaches to caching, several ways of building search indexes, and so on. When building an application, we still need to figure out which tools and which approaches are the most appropriate for the task at hand. And it can be hard to combine tools when you need to do something that a single tool cannot do alone. Thi

instruction: Why does the author switch to discussing data streaming in the third part of the book?
output: —Donald Knuth In the first two parts of this book we talked a lot about requests and queries, and the corresponding responses or results. This style of data processing is assumed in many modern data systems: you ask for something, or you send an instruction, and some time later the system (hopefully) gives you an answer.

This style of data processing is assumed in many modern data systems: you ask for something, or you send an instruction, and some time later the system (hopefully) gives you an answer.

instruction: What types of systems respond to requests from other systems instead of human users?
output: Databases, caches, search indexes, web servers, and many other systems work this way. In such online systems, whether it’s a web browser requesting a page or a service call‐ ing a remote API, we generally assume that the request is triggered by a human user, and that the user is waiting for the response.

instruction: Which system is an example of a service that responds to requests from other systems?
output: Databases, caches, search indexes, web servers, and many other systems work this way. In such online systems, whether it’s a web browser requesting a page or a service call‐ ing a remote API, we generally assume that the request is triggered by a human user, and that the user is waiting for the response.

instruction: How does a payment processor system receive requests from a merchant's system?
output: Databases, caches, search indexes, web servers, and many other systems work this way. In such online systems, whether it’s a web browser requesting a page or a service call‐ ing a remote API, we generally assume that the request is triggered by a human user, and that the user is waiting for the response.

instruction: Why does Amazon use request/response for its search functionality?
output: They shouldn’t have to wait too long, so we pay a lot of attention to the response time of these systems (see “Describing Perfor‐ mance” on page 13). The web, and increasing numbers of HTTP/REST-based APIs, has made the request/ response style of interaction so common that it’s easy to take it for granted.

instruction: How long does it take for the service to respond to a request?
output: When one is received, the service tries to handle it as quickly as possible and sends a response back. Response time is usually the primary measure of performance of a service, and availability is often very important (if the client can’t reach the service, the user will probably get an error message).

instruction: What is the importance of response time and availability for a service?
output: When one is received, the service tries to handle it as quickly as possible and sends a response back. Response time is usually the primary measure of performance of a service, and availability is often very important (if the client can’t reach the service, the user will probably get an error message).

instruction: Why does the service prioritize handling a request as soon as it is received?

Response time is usually the primary measure of performance of a service, and availability is often very important (if the client can’t reach the service, the user will probably get an error message).

instruction: What type of system processes large amounts of input data without a user present?
output: 389 Batch processing systems (offline systems) A batch processing system takes a large amount of input data, runs a job to pro‐ cess it, and produces some output data. Jobs often take a while (from a few minutes to several days), so there normally isn’t a user waiting for the job to fin‐ ish.

instruction: What is the output data of a batch processing system that processes claims for an insurance company?
output: 389 Batch processing systems (offline systems) A batch processing system takes a large amount of input data, runs a job to pro‐ cess it, and produces some output data. Jobs often take a while (from a few minutes to several days), so there normally isn’t a user waiting for the job to fin‐ ish.

instruction: How is the data checked for errors in the insurance company's batch processing system?
output: 389 Batch processing systems (offline systems) A batch processing system takes a large amount of input data, runs a job to pro‐ cess it, and produces some output data. Jobs often take a while (from a few minutes to several days), so there normally isn’t a user waiting for the job to fin‐ ish.

instruction: Why does adding a third CPU result in less processing time reduction than the second CPU?
output: Instead, batch jobs are often scheduled to run periodically (for example, once a day). The primary performance measure of a batch job is usually throughput (the time it takes to crunch through an input dataset of a certain size).

instruction: How much processing time reduction can be expected when adding a third CPU to a batch job?
output: Instead, batch jobs are often scheduled to run periodically (for example, once a day). The primary performance measure of a batch job is usually throughput (the time it takes to crunch through an input dataset of a certain size).

instruction: What is the term for the overhead involved when assigning tasks to multiple CPUs in a batch job?
output: Instead, batch jobs are often scheduled to run periodically (for example, once a day). The primary performance measure of a batch job is usually throughput (the time it takes to crunch through an input dataset of a certain size).

instruction: How does stream processing differ from batch processing?
output: We dis‐ cuss batch processing in this chapter. Stream processing systems (near-real-time systems) Stream processing is somewhere between online and offline/batch processing (so it is sometimes called near-real-time or nearline processing).

instruction: What is the difference between near-real-time and real-time systems?
output: We dis‐ cuss batch processing in this chapter. Stream processing systems (near-real-time systems) Stream processing is somewhere between online and offline/batch processing (so it is sometimes called near-real-time or nearline processing).

instruction: Why is stream processing considered to be between online and offline processing?
output: We dis‐ cuss batch processing in this chapter. Stream processing systems (near-real-time systems) Stream processing is somewhere between online and offline/batch processing (so it is sometimes called near-real-time or nearline processing).

instruction: Why does a stream processor consume inputs and produce outputs like a batch processing system?
output: Like a batch pro‐ cessing system, a stream processor consumes inputs and produces outputs (rather than responding to requests). However, a stream job operates on events shortly after they happen, whereas a batch job operates on a fixed set of input data.

instruction: How does a stream processor differ from a batch processing system in terms of input data?
output: Like a batch pro‐ cessing system, a stream processor consumes inputs and produces outputs (rather than responding to requests). However, a stream job operates on events shortly after they happen, whereas a batch job operates on a fixed set of input data.

instruction: What kind of data does a stream processor process?
output: Like a batch pro‐ cessing system, a stream processor consumes inputs and produces outputs (rather than responding to requests). However, a stream job operates on events shortly after they happen, whereas a batch job operates on a fixed set of input data.

instruction: Why is the latency lower in stream processing systems compared to batch systems?
output: This difference allows stream processing systems to have lower latency than the equivalent batch systems. As stream processing builds upon batch process‐ ing, we discuss it in Chapter 11. As we shall see in this chapter, batch processing is an important building block in our quest to build reliable, scalable, and maintainable applications.

instruction: How does stream processing build upon batch processing?

As we shall see in this chapter, batch processing is an important building block in our quest to build reliable, scalable, and maintainable applications.

instruction: What algorithm was described as making Google massively scalable in 2004?
output: For example, Map‐ Reduce, a batch processing algorithm published in 2004 [1], was (perhaps overenthusiastically) called “the algorithm that makes Google so massively scalable” [2]. It was subsequently implemented in various open source data systems, including Hadoop, CouchDB, and MongoDB.

instruction: How did Map-Reduce get its name and why was it implemented in open source data systems like Hadoop, CouchDB, and MongoDB?
output: For example, Map‐ Reduce, a batch processing algorithm published in 2004 [1], was (perhaps overenthusiastically) called “the algorithm that makes Google so massively scalable” [2]. It was subsequently implemented in various open source data systems, including Hadoop, CouchDB, and MongoDB.

instruction: What year was Map-Reduce first published?
output: For example, Map‐ Reduce, a batch processing algorithm published in 2004 [1], was (perhaps overenthusiastically) called “the algorithm that makes Google so massively scalable” [2]. It was subsequently implemented in various open source data systems, including Hadoop, CouchDB, and MongoDB.

instruction: What is MapReduce compared to in terms of programming model level?
output: MapReduce is a fairly low-level programming model compared to the parallel pro‐ cessing systems that were developed for data warehouses many years previously [3, 4], but it was a major step forward in terms of the scale of processing that could be achieved on commodity hardware.

instruction: Why was MapReduce a major step forward in terms of processing scale?
output: MapReduce is a fairly low-level programming model compared to the parallel pro‐ cessing systems that were developed for data warehouses many years previously [3, 4], but it was a major step forward in terms of the scale of processing that could be achieved on commodity hardware.

instruction: How did MapReduce enable larger processing scales on commodity hardware compared to previous data warehousing systems?
output: MapReduce is a fairly low-level programming model compared to the parallel pro‐ cessing systems that were developed for data warehouses many years previously [3, 4], but it was a major step forward in terms of the scale of processing that could be achieved on commodity hardware.

instruction: Why was MapReduce introduced?
output: Although the importance of MapReduce is now declining [5], it is still worth understanding, because it provides a clear picture of why and how batch processing is useful. In fact, batch processing is a very old form of computing.

instruction: What are the two main functions of MapReduce?
output: Although the importance of MapReduce is now declining [5], it is still worth understanding, because it provides a clear picture of why and how batch processing is useful. In fact, batch processing is a very old form of computing.

instruction: How is MapReduce used for processing large text data?

instruction: How were statistics computed using punch card tabulating machines before digital computers?
output: Long before programmable digital computers were invented, punch card tabulating machines—such as the Hol‐ lerith machines used in the 1890 US Census [6]—implemented a semi-mechanized form of batch processing to compute aggregate statistics from large inputs.

instruction: What machines were used for semi-mechanized batch processing before the invention of digital computers?
output: Long before programmable digital computers were invented, punch card tabulating machines—such as the Hol‐ lerith machines used in the 1890 US Census [6]—implemented a semi-mechanized form of batch processing to compute aggregate statistics from large inputs.

instruction: Why were punch card tabulating machines used for batch processing statistics before digital computers?
output: Long before programmable digital computers were invented, punch card tabulating machines—such as the Hol‐ lerith machines used in the 1890 US Census [6]—implemented a semi-mechanized form of batch processing to compute aggregate statistics from large inputs.

instruction: What were the IBM card-sorting machines used for in the 1940s and 1950s?
output: And Map‐ Reduce bears an uncanny resemblance to the electromechanical IBM card-sorting machines that were widely used for business data processing in the 1940s and 1950s [7]. As usual, history has a tendency of repeating itself.

instruction: How did the IBM card-sorting machines inspire the development of Map-Reduce?
output: And Map‐ Reduce bears an uncanny resemblance to the electromechanical IBM card-sorting machines that were widely used for business data processing in the 1940s and 1950s [7]. As usual, history has a tendency of repeating itself.

instruction: What information can be extracted from the given line of an nginx access log?
output: For example, using the nginx default access log format, one line of the log might look like this: 216.58.210.78 - - [27/Feb/2015:17:55:11 +0000] "GET /css/typography.css HTTP/1.1" 200 3377 "http://martin.kleppmann.com/" "Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_9_5) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/40.0.2214.115 Safari/537.36" (That is actually one line; it’s only broken onto multiple lines here for readability.)

instruction: Why does the line in the nginx access log contain the IP address 216.58.210.78?
output: For example, using the nginx default access log format, one line of the log might look like this: 216.58.210.78 - - [27/Feb/2015:17:55:11 +0000] "GET /css/typography.css HTTP/1.1" 200 3377 "http://martin.kleppmann.com/" "Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_9_5) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/40.0.2214.115 Safari/537.36" (That is actually one line; it’s only broken onto multiple lines here for readability.)

instruction: How can you determine the date and time from the log entry " [27/Feb/2015:17:55:11 +0000]"?
output: For example, using the nginx default access log format, one line of the log might look like this: 216.58.210.78 - - [27/Feb/2015:17:55:11 +0000] "GET /css/typography.css HTTP/1.1" 200 3377 "http://martin.kleppmann.com/" "Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_9_5) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/40.0.2214.115 Safari/537.36" (That is actually one line; it’s only broken onto multiple lines here for readability.)

instruction: What is the date and time indicated in the log?
output: There’s a lot of information in that line. In order to interpret it, you need to look at the definition of the log format, which is as follows: $remote_addr - $remote_user [$time_local] "$request" $status $body_bytes_sent "$http_referer" "$http_user_agent" So, this one line of the log indicates that on February 27, 2015, at 17:55:11 UTC, the server received a request for the file /css/typography.css from the client IP address 216.58.210.78.

instruction: What does the log entry for this request indicate about the client IP address?
output: There’s a lot of information in that line. In order to interpret it, you need to look at the definition of the log format, which is as follows: $remote_addr - $remote_user [$time_local] "$request" $status $body_bytes_sent "$http_referer" "$http_user_agent" So, this one line of the log indicates that on February 27, 2015, at 17:55:11 UTC, the server received a request for the file /css/typography.css from the client IP address 216.58.210.78.

instruction: What process occurs when the server receives a request for a file like /css/typography.css?
output: There’s a lot of information in that line. In order to interpret it, you need to look at the definition of the log format, which is as follows: $remote_addr - $remote_user [$time_local] "$request" $status $body_bytes_sent "$http_referer" "$http_user_agent" So, this one line of the log indicates that on February 27, 2015, at 17:55:11 UTC, the server received a request for the file /css/typography.css from the client IP address 216.58.210.78.

instruction: What value is assigned to $remote_user when the user is not authenticated?
output: The user was not authenticated, so $remote_user is set to a hyphen (-). The response status was 200 (i.e., the request was successful), and the response was 3,377 bytes in size.

instruction: Why is the response status 200, even though the user is not authenticated?
output: The user was not authenticated, so $remote_user is set to a hyphen (-). The response status was 200 (i.e., the request was successful), and the response was 3,377 bytes in size.

instruction: What is the command to extract the 7th column from a Unix log file?
output: For example, say you want to find the five most popular pages on your website. You can do this in a Unix shell as follows:i cat /var/log/nginx/access.log | awk '{print $7}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -r -n | head -n 5 Read the log file.

instruction: What is the purpose of the -c option in the uniq command?
output: The uniq command filters out repeated lines in its input by checking whether two adjacent lines are the same. The -c option tells it to also output a counter: for every distinct URL, it reports how many times that URL appeared in the input.

instruction: How does the uniq command handle repeated lines in its input?

instruction: What kind of files can be excluded from the report using the awk command?
output: It will process gigabytes of log files in a matter of seconds, and you can easily modify the analysis to suit your needs. For example, if you want to omit CSS files from the report, change the awk argument to '$7 !~ /\.css$/ {print $7}'.

instruction: What data structure is used in the example to count the occurrences of each URL in the log file?
output: For example, in Ruby, it might look something like this: 392 | Chapter 10: Batch Processing counts = Hash.new(0) File.open('/var/log/nginx/access.log') do |file| file.each do |line| url = line.split[6] counts[url] += 1 end end top5 = counts.map{|url, count| [count, url] }.sort.reverse[0...5] top5.each{|count, url| puts "#{count} #{url}" } counts is a hash table that keeps a counter for the number of times we’ve seen each URL.

instruction: Why is the counts hash table initialized with the zero value in the example?
output: For example, in Ruby, it might look something like this: 392 | Chapter 10: Batch Processing counts = Hash.new(0) File.open('/var/log/nginx/access.log') do |file| file.each do |line| url = line.split[6] counts[url] += 1 end end top5 = counts.map{|url, count| [count, url] }.sort.reverse[0...5] top5.each{|count, url| puts "#{count} #{url}" } counts is a hash table that keeps a counter for the number of times we’ve seen each URL.

instruction: What is the default state of a counter in the context of the log?
output: A counter is zero by default. From each line of the log, we take the URL to be the seventh whitespaceseparated field (the array index here is 6 because Ruby’s arrays are zero-indexed).

instruction: What is the action taken to determine the top five URLs from the log?
output: Increment the counter for the URL in the current line of the log. Sort the hash table contents by counter value (descending), and take the top five entries. Print out those top five entries.
